items.q: Does a falling share of factory jobs mean a country's manufacturing has collapsed?
items.correct: Not necessarily — mechanisation lets output rise while employment falls, and the share can also drop simply because services are growing faster.
items.misconception: Fewer manufacturing workers always means less manufacturing output.
items.random_wrong: Factory jobs disappear because factories close every winter.
items.why: Students read employment percentages as output; productivity growth breaks that link.

items.q: Is the informal sector simply a problem to be eliminated?
items.correct: It avoids taxes and worker protections, but it also provides vital incomes, goods and services where formal jobs are scarce, so cities usually aim to manage and gradually formalise it.
items.misconception: Informal work has no value to a city's economy.
items.random_wrong: The informal sector means people working in casual clothes.
items.why: One-sided views of the informal economy miss its livelihoods role in LIC cities.

items.correct: No — it is low-carbon but relies on finite uranium, so it is classed as non-renewable; renewability and carbon emissions are different ideas.
items.misconception: Because nuclear power is low-carbon it must be renewable.
items.random_wrong: Nuclear power is generated from recycled batteries.
items.why: Students conflate 'clean' or 'low-carbon' with 'renewable'; the distinction is regularly examined.

items.q: Which of these is a primary sector activity?
items.why: It's Fishing. “Car manufacturing” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: As a country industrialises, employment typically:
items.correct: Shifts from the primary sector into secondary and tertiary work
items.misconception: Shifts from services back into farming
items.why: Correct answer: Shifts from the primary sector into secondary and tertiary work. “Shifts from services back into farming” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's Natural gas. “Wind” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: The informal sector is best described as:
items.correct: Unregulated work outside official records, such as street trading
items.why: Correct answer: Unregulated work outside official records, such as street trading. “Government employment” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A country has 12 million workers, of whom 3 million work in the primary sector. What percentage work in the primary sector?
items.why: The real figure is 25 %. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
